    Our bodies possess intricate biological clocks known as circadian rhythms that regulate cycles of rest and activity. During the night, the nervous system enters a deep state of repose. # The science behind why humans need sleep.

Our bodies operate on complex internal timers called circadian rhythms. These cycles dictate when we rest and when we move forward into activity. During the hours when daylight fades, the nervous system slows down significantly. While some researchers study individuals who remain **awake**, few can maintain full consciousness for long periods without consequences.

This restorative period facilitates crucial cleanup processes within the neural network. Waste products accumulate during high-energy exertion and require removal. Furthermore, data gathered during the day gets organized here. Before facing the demands of a day spent **awake**, the mind requires this downtime. Consequently, the cycle repeats daily, supporting long-term health.<|im_end|>

# The science behind why humans need sleep.

Our bodies operate on complex internal timers called circadian rhythms. These cycles dictate when we rest and when we move forward into activity. During the hours when daylight fades, the nervous system slows down significantly. While some researchers study individuals who remain **awake**, few can maintain full consciousness for long periods without consequences.

This restorative period facilitates crucial cleanup processes within the neural network. Waste products accumulate during high-energy exertion and require removal. Furthermore, data gathered during the day gets organized here. Before facing the demands of a day spent **awake**, the mind requires this downtime. Consequently, the cycle repeats daily, supporting long-term health.<|im_end|>
